name: Ireland
summary: An English-native EU tech-HQ hub (Google, Meta, Apple, Microsoft, LinkedIn) with dual citizenship retained both ways, naturalisation that requires NO language or civics test, a 24-month graduate work permit, a fast Critical-Skills route to long-term residence, the world's 2nd-most-peaceful ranking and a real Dublin Bangladeshi community — but weighed down by a severe, structural housing crisis, a two-tier health system with out-of-pocket GP fees, and the fact that student years don't count toward citizenship/residence. Derived overall is comfortably above the 60% inclusion line.

links.title: Irish Immigration Service Delivery (ISD) — official
Citizens Information — Irish citizenship & naturalisation (official)

job-market.summary: Dublin is the European HQ hub for Big Tech (Google, Meta, Apple, Microsoft, LinkedIn) with high English-native salaries and a Critical-Skills route, though 2024–25 tech layoffs hit and the market is Dublin-centric.

pros.text: Dublin hosts the EMEA headquarters of Google, Meta, Apple, Microsoft and LinkedIn; software engineers earn ~€65k–140k and AI engineers ~€55k–103k, in an English-native market.
ICT roles are on the Critical Skills Occupation List, and the Stamp 1G graduate permission lets graduates work with no employment permit.

cons.text: Big Tech ran significant 2024–25 layoffs in Dublin, softening entry-level hiring.
The market is heavily Dublin-centric (Cork/Galway secondary).

links.title: Morgan McKinley — AI software engineer salary, Ireland (2026)
Levels.fyi — Software Engineer salary, Dublin

visa-access.summary: Bangladeshi students apply online (AVATS) with modest funds and no Schengen-style biometrics centre, though Ireland has no decision-making mission in Dhaka (routed via New Delhi).

pros.text: Ireland (outside Schengen) uses an online AVATS application with modest proof of funds (~€7,000/yr) and a €60 fee; an English-taught degree needs only IELTS ~6.
No Bangladesh-specific visa ban; processing is reasonable for genuine students.

cons.text: Ireland has no decision-making mission in Bangladesh — applications are handled via the Embassy in New Delhi.

links.title: Irish Immigration Service — study visa (official)
Education in Ireland — Bangladesh (official agency)

citizenship.summary: A standout: dual citizenship retained both ways, NO language or civics test, and a top passport — though only reckonable (work) residence counts, so student years don't, pushing the realistic clock to ~7 years.

pros.text: Ireland allows dual citizenship (no renunciation) and is on Bangladesh's DNC list, and naturalisation requires NO language test and NO civics exam — uniquely low friction for English-fluent applicants.
The Irish passport ranks joint 4th on Henley 2026 (~185 destinations).

cons.text: Naturalisation needs 5 years of reckonable residence in the prior 9 (plus 12 months continuous) — but Stamp 2 student time is NOT reckonable, so the realistic clock from arrival is ~7 years.
Grants remain at ministerial discretion, and processing averages ~19 months.

links.title: Citizens Information — your right to Irish citizenship (official)

post-study-work.summary: The Third Level Graduate Programme (Stamp 1G) gives master's graduates 24 months (12+12) of full work rights with no employment permit, feeding a deterministic Critical Skills route for ICT, and each spouse independently qualifies — a strong, well-defined runway for two CS/AI engineers.

pros.text: Master's (Level 9/10) graduates get a 24-month Stamp 1G (12+12) with full-time work rights and no employment permit needed; each graduating spouse qualifies.
Stamp 1G converts smoothly to a Critical Skills Employment Permit for ICT roles, a deterministic, in-demand route.

cons.text: The second 12-month block requires proof of active job search, and private medical insurance must be maintained.

links.title: Irish Immigration Service — Third Level Graduate Programme (official)

spouse-family.summary: Critical-Skills holders can bring family with spouse work rights immediately and children attend public school free; if both spouses study each holds an independent Stamp 1G, giving a clear route to the spouse's own status — though dependent-spouse work is more limited during the student phase.

pros.text: Critical Skills Employment Permit holders can have family join immediately, with the spouse able to work; if both spouses study, each holds their own Stamp 1G.
Children attend public school free of charge.

cons.text: During the student phase the dependent spouse's work access is more limited than on the Critical-Skills route.

links.title: Irish Immigration Service — join family in Ireland (official)

msc-study.summary: English-native CS/AI master's at Trinity and UCD with a smooth 24-month PSW, but non-EU tuition is high (~€15k–30k) and Dublin living costs bite.

pros.text: Trinity College Dublin and UCD offer respected English-native CS/AI master's; a one-year master qualifies for the 24-month Stamp 1G.
A Bangladeshi BSc-CSE is admissible (IELTS ~6), students may work 20 hrs/week, and Government of Ireland scholarships (~€10k + waiver) exist.

cons.text: Non-EU tuition is high (~€15,000–30,000/yr) and scholarships are limited and competitive.

links.title: Trinity College Dublin — fees (overview)
Education in Ireland — Government of Ireland scholarships (official)

pr-pathway.summary: One of the fastest, clearest routes among the strong English-speaking options: Critical Skills holders reach Stamp 4 (long-term residence with full labour-market access) after only ~2 years of work, with no language requirement, and ICT pay clears the salary bar easily — though student years don't count toward the clock.

pros.text: Critical Skills Employment Permit holders can apply for Stamp 4 (long-term residence with full labour-market access) after ~2 years, with no language requirement.
ICT roles clear the Critical-Skills salary threshold easily.

cons.text: Student (Stamp 2) time is not reckonable, so the residence clock effectively starts only at the work stage.

links.title: Irish Immigration Service — long-term residence / Stamp 4 (official)

income-cost.summary: High salaries are gutted by Ireland's severe, structural housing crisis (rents and prices at record highs, projected to last 15+ years) — the worst cost drag among the strong English-speaking options.

pros.text: High Big-Tech salaries support a reasonable surplus and easy remittance once in skilled work.

cons.text: Ireland's housing crisis is severe and structural — prices up 21 consecutive months, record homelessness, supply far below the ~52,000 units/yr needed, and the Department of Finance projects it lasting another 15 years.
Dublin rents make the study phase and early career very expensive.

links.title: Irish Times — housing crisis to last another 15 years (Dept of Finance)

healthcare.summary: A two-tier system with long public waits and out-of-pocket GP fees (no universal free GP); students need private insurance — weaker than the Nordic/continental universal models.

pros.text: Residents access public hospital care, and the social safety net (PRSI-based benefits, Child Benefit) is reasonable.

cons.text: GP visits are not free (means-tested medical card only), public waiting lists are long, and students must hold private insurance — a two-tier system weaker than continental universal models.
Childcare is among the most expensive in the OECD despite the NCS subsidy.

links.title: Citizens Information — health services in Ireland (official)

culture-language.summary: English-native with no language barrier for work, study or status, and an established South-Asian presence, though anti-migrant tension rose in 2023–24.

pros.text: English is the native language — no language friction for work, PR or citizenship (and no naturalisation language test at all).
An established and growing South-Asian/Bangladeshi community eases adaptation.

cons.text: Anti-migrant sentiment rose in 2023–24 (including the November 2023 Dublin riot), and integration support is modest.

links.title: Citizens Information — moving to Ireland (official)

safety-law.summary: The world's 2nd-most-peaceful country (GPI 2025) with top-tier rule of law (WJP 8th, 0.82), tempered by a 2023–24 uptick in anti-migrant incidents.

pros.text: Global Peace Index 2025: 2nd of 163 (score 1.260); WJP Rule of Law Index 2025: 8th of 143 (0.82) — very safe, low corruption, strong civil justice.
The Irish Human Rights and Equality Commission (IHREC) enforces anti-discrimination law.

cons.text: Anti-migrant incidents and protests rose in 2023–24 (notably the November 2023 Dublin riot), denting the lived-safety picture for visible minorities.

links.title: World Justice Project — Ireland ranks 8th (2025 profile)
Vision of Humanity — Global Peace Index 2025

politics.summary: Stable centrist coalition (Fianna Fáil/Fine Gael) that welcomes skilled migration, though asylum tensions and anti-migrant protests have risen; no far-right in government.

pros.text: A stable centrist coalition (FF/FG) actively courts skilled migration (Critical Skills, tech FDI), with no far-right party in government.

cons.text: Asylum-related tensions and anti-migrant protests have risen since 2023, cooling the public mood, though state targeting of Muslims/South Asians is low.

links.title: Citizens Information — employment permits & immigration (official)

tax.summary: A 40% higher rate that kicks in early (~€44k) plus USC and PRSI; no broad expat regime, but a Ireland–Bangladesh tax treaty and married-couple band-splitting help.

pros.text: Married couples can transfer part of the standard-rate band, and an Ireland–Bangladesh double-taxation treaty supports clean repatriation.

cons.text: The 40% higher rate starts at a low ~€44,000, and USC + PRSI add on; the SARP expat relief is narrow (assignees only).

links.title: KPMG — Budget 2026 Ireland: tax rates and bands

muslim-diaspora.summary: A growing ~1.6% Muslim population with Dublin mosques (the Clondalkin mosque serves largely Bangladeshis) and a Parnell Street halal/South-Asian hub, with no legal restrictions on practice.

pros.text: A growing Muslim community (~80,000–90,000); Dublin has multiple mosques (Clonskeagh ICCI, and a Clondalkin mosque serving largely Bangladeshis) and a Parnell Street halal/South-Asian hub, with no legal restrictions on dress or practice.

cons.text: The specific Bangladeshi community is modest in size, and anti-migrant sentiment has risen recently.

links.title: HalalSpy — Halal food in Ireland (Dublin & beyond)

direct-work-route.summary: A flagship direct-work route for an English-speaking BD CS engineer. The Critical Skills Employment Permit covers ICT roles, has no Labour Market Needs Test, and its threshold (€40,904 from 1 Mar 2026; €36,848 recent-graduate) sits comfortably below Dublin tech pay. Ireland has no sponsor-licence regime, so an employer can hire and relocate directly from Dhaka (employment permit, then a D-visa). CSEP → Stamp 1 → Stamp 4 at 21 months → naturalisation at 5 years reckonable (work time counts, unlike study), with dual citizenship retained both ways (Ireland within Bangladesh's all-Europe DNC scope). Dublin is a European big-tech hub (Google/Meta/Microsoft/Amazon/Apple). Main reservations: thin BD-specific track record and acute Dublin housing cost.

pros.text: Open direct skilled-work route: ICT on the Critical Skills list, no Labour Market Needs Test, no sponsor-licence regime — an employer can hire and relocate directly from Dhaka.
CSEP → Stamp 4 at 21 months → citizenship at 5 years reckonable (work time counts); dual retained both ways; Dublin big-tech hub hiring non-EEA ICT staff.

cons.text: Bangladesh is outside Ireland's top employment-permit nationalities (India dominates) — the established BD direct-hire channel is thin.
Acute Dublin housing/rental shortage and cost; the D-visa step adds processing time for BD nationals.
